content: It does have a spinlock in it.

Boqun Feng said:

Additional question: should XArray's APIs take Pin<&Self>?

Oh, I had missed the fact that it has a spinlock in it. This needs to be pinned.
But actually "unlocked spinlock is safe to move", or a boarder question, can Unpin be built from some !Unpins?

Boqun Feng said:

But actually "unlocked spinlock is safe to move", or a boarder question, can Unpin be built from some !Unpins?

Oh, I guess we can still make XArray take Pin<&Self> but impl Unpin for XArray {}?

Boqun Feng said:

But actually "unlocked spinlock is safe to move", or a boarder question, can Unpin be built from some !Unpins?

In the RT case, a spinlock is a mutex. A mutex has a list_head in it, so moving is unsound even when unlocked, no?

(I suppose it's ok for non-RT in the archs we know spinlocks don't have self-references when unlocked, but I unless I'm missing something, it's not universally safe.)

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Boqun Feng said:

But actually "unlocked spinlock is safe to move", or a boarder question, can Unpin be built from some !Unpins?

In the RT case, a spinlock is a mutex. A mutex has a list_head in it, so moving is unsound even when unlocked, no?

(I suppose it's ok for non-RT in the archs we know spinlocks don't have self-references when unlocked, but I unless I'm missing something, it's not universally safe.)

Good point on the RT case
However, rt_mutex now uses a rbtree instead of a list to record waiters, so.. still movable I think.

Boqun Feng said:

However, rt_mutex now uses a rbtree instead of a list to record waiters, so.. still movable I think.

Implementation detail that I'm not sure we should depend on. Raw spinlocks are also arch-dependent, x86 would be fine to move when unlocked, but we haven't checked all archs to confirm this, and even if did, I don't think at this stage that we can mandate that they don't introduce self-references, so it would be possible for some arch to introduce them in the future.
Except that raw_spinlock has to be 32bit, so it's very hard for it to self-reference, but it's possible.
Where does the 32bit size requirement come from?

In the tip tree, MCS locks are used in the implementation of mutexes, but they do not replace the existing ticket spinlock implementation. One reason for this is size: ticket spinlocks fit into a single 32-bit word, while MCS locks do not. That turns out to be important: spinlocks are embedded into many kernel structures, some of which (notably struct page) cannot tolerate an increase in size. If the MCS lock technique is to be used throughout the kernel, some other approach will be needed.
9 messages were moved here from #General > Pin<_> in method signature by Boqun Feng.
A message was moved here from #General > Pin<_> in method signature by Boqun Feng.

Boqun Feng said:

But actually "unlocked spinlock is safe to move", or a boarder question, can Unpin be built from some !Unpins?

You can theoretically do something like this:

struct Foo {
    spinlock: Spinlock<()>,
    mtx: Mutex<()>,
}

// Unpin is a safe trait
impl Unpin for Foo {}

fn main() {
    let mut foo = Foo { /*somehow initialize the two fields without pinning them*/ };
    let s = &mut foo.spinlock;
    // we now have a `&mut Spinlock<()>`, but we can never lock it, since that requires pinning and we are not allowed to do that, since `Foo: Unpin`
}
At the moment XArray is not pinned and even has the safety comment "This data structure does not requiring pinning".
